content:
What AI-assisted work frees up is not just time, but attention for judgment, direction, and better next decisions.
I keep noticing a shift
Over the last few weeks, I have noticed a change in the way I work with AI-assisted development tools. At first, I found it hard to name.
Individual tasks become faster. Of course it helps when an agent writes boilerplate, creates files, runs tests, finds errors, or builds an initial project structure. That is useful. It saves time. But the more interesting effect happens somewhere else: while the implementation is already moving, more attention is left for the question of what the next meaningful step should be.
I am still working on the technical task in front of me. But I see earlier which product decision sits behind it. I notice sooner whether a structure is likely to hold. I start thinking about the next idea while the current one no longer occupies all of my mental bandwidth.
AI saves time and changes where attention is tied up.
That sounds abstract. In practice, it feels very concrete. Part of the work still remains mine: defining the goal, holding the direction, checking intermediate results, assessing quality, making the next decision. But part of the translation work becomes lighter.
By translation work, I mean all those small steps between intention and a first usable result: syntax, file paths, configuration, framework details, documentation lookup, small corrections, formatting, repetition. All of that is legitimate work. All of it matters. It also takes up a lot of thinking space.
When some of that thinking space becomes free, something interesting happens.
The bottleneck moves
In the past, my bottleneck in development was often close to implementation. I had to hold enough technical detail in my head to reach a first usable state at all: Which file? Which API? Which type? Which component? Which configuration? Which side effect?
With an AI agent, this point moves. I can describe a goal, set a few rules, show the existing context, and let it build a first path. After that, the real work often starts earlier: Is this the right structure? Does it fit the project? Is the abstraction useful? Will it remain maintainable? Does it match the intention? The bottleneck moves.
“How do I build this?” turns into “What should I actually build?” much earlier.
That is the more important productivity effect for me. If we only measure AI by how many minutes it saves on a task, we see part of the value. The other part lies in the shift of attention. More mental energy moves toward selection, evaluation, architecture, and product logic.
This shift is not always comfortable. Once implementation becomes easier, your own decisions become more visible. You can produce much more, much faster. That makes the ability to stop, sort, and decide more important.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI speeds up implementation and makes earlier decisions more visible.Abstraction has done this before
We know this pattern from the history of technology. A calculator reduces the effort of mental arithmetic and makes more complex calculations accessible. A compiler reduces the need to think close to the machine. Frameworks reduce repeated infrastructure work. Cloud services shift attention away from individual servers toward availability, scaling, and operating models. Each abstraction has accelerated work and changed the place where humans need to think.
With AI, this shift is broader. It touches language, structure, research, programming, writing, planning, and review. That creates a different effect from many earlier tools: the boundary between executing, thinking along, and suggesting becomes softer.
An AI agent waits for the next instruction and at the same time interprets intent, pulls in context, makes suggestions, builds intermediate steps, and pre-formulates decisions. That can be enormously helpful. It can also move very plausibly in a direction I did not really want.
The more a tool thinks along, the more important my own judgment becomes.
That is where the new tension sits for me.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Technical abstractions do not only save time. They shift the level at which humans think.A concrete example from my current work
One example is a methods and knowledge project I am currently working on. From the outside, that may sound like catalog work: collect methods, write descriptions, assign categories, document sources, prepare visuals. In practice, it contains far more judgment than it first appears.
A method is easy to describe. The harder question is whether the description actually holds. What is the method useful for? In which context does it fit? Which assumptions are built into it? Which sources are reliable? Where are its limits? What alternatives exist? Which method belongs before or after it?
AI helps me get to a first working state faster in a project like this. It can structure rough drafts, suggest comparison questions, sort variants, formulate image ideas, or turn a loose note into a usable first system. That gives me something to examine, move around, shorten, reject, or develop further.
The actual work begins exactly there. As soon as a first draft is visible, I have to decide whether the weighting is right. Whether one method appears too important even though it is only a side aspect. Whether a visual really shows the form of thinking or merely looks nice. Whether a category creates orientation or narrows the topic too early. Whether a recommendation is justified or only sounds plausible. Implementation becomes visible sooner. So do the conceptual decisions.
Freed capacity creates follow-up ideas
The most surprising effect for me is that the next idea often appears while I am still working on the current one. I work on a concrete artifact and notice the next questions opening up in parallel. A method collection turns into the question of decision support. A description turns into the question of context. A sketch turns into the question of which movement of thought an image should actually show. A small LinkedIn draft turns into a possible Medium article about AI, attention, and new bottlenecks.
Sometimes this almost feels like cognitive parallelization. One part of me is still evaluating the current result. Another part is already thinking about structure, reuse, follow-up, or the next product idea.
Of course, this can become noisy. Once too many follow-up ideas appear at the same time, selection becomes necessary again. That is why the shift of the bottleneck matters so much: AI does not automatically create better direction. It creates more movement. Direction has to be made out of that movement.
When implementation becomes easier, selection becomes more valuable.
This is something I miss in many productivity discussions about AI. They often focus on speed, output, or automation. For my work, another question is becoming more important: Which kind of mental work becomes free, and what do I use it for?
The new bottleneck is judgment
The more AI takes over, the more I notice how important domain-aware judgment becomes. An agent can write a draft. I have to notice whether the tone is right. It can build a structure. I have to assess whether it fits the project. It can propose a technical solution. I have to evaluate risks, maintainability, and product logic. It can formulate a plausible explanation. I have to recognize whether the weighting is right.
That is the core of the new division of labor. AI makes many things reachable sooner. As a result, the question of quality moves forward. It accompanies the work continuously. Every acceleration creates more need for decision.
For me, domain knowledge therefore becomes more important. Someone who does not understand the work can create results faster and judge them worse. Someone who understands the work can use AI to reach the interesting layers sooner: structure, selection, evaluation, direction.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The new division of labor makes human judgment more central.Productivity as shifted attention
Maybe we should think about AI productivity less as pure time savings. Time savings are real. They are pleasant. They make many tasks easier. But they only explain part of why the work feels different.
For me, the deeper effect is shifted attention. Less energy goes into the manual translation from goal to first result. More energy can flow into the question of which goal actually matters. Which structure will still work later. Which decision is really at stake. Which idea only sounds attractive and which one has substance.
That is a significant change. It makes work faster, but also denser. More possibilities become visible earlier. More decisions are in the room at the same time. More responsibility lands with the person who gives direction.
AI shifts what we can think about earlier.
That is why I find this moment so interesting. A new layer of work becomes reachable earlier. Maybe that is the real productivity effect: when implementation ties up less attention, more room remains for judgment, direction, and good follow-up decisions. And that is where the actual work begins for me right now.
